name: Hard Candy Bswn00a
recipeInstructions:
1. Have the oil flavor/flavors, citric acid, and thermometer ready when you start cooking.
Some flavors are stronger than others.
Add citric acid to fruit-flavored candies for a tart taste.
2. Test your thermometer by placing it in cold water and bringing it to a boil. If it boils at 210°F instead of 212°F, reduce the cooking time by 2 degrees.
3. Spray the molds and surface with Pam (PAM), not too much, and only once per session.
4. Use a thin pan; do not use a heavy one. The candy must set for a few minutes in the pan, and the temperature will rise as it cooks, potentially burning the candy in a thick pan.
5. Bring the mixture to a boil.
6. Put the lid on for 3 minutes (to wash down sugar crystals), then add the thermometer without stirring.
7. When the thermometer reaches 285-290°F, remove the pot from the stove.
8. Wait for the temperature to drop below 260°F before adding the oil flavor/flavors and citric acid.
> The flavors will cook out if added too hot.
9. Cover the pan after adding citric acid so it doesn't fade the taste. Add about 1 teaspoon (or more) of citric acid.
10. Use small amounts of Lorann oils, available in cake supply shops or drug stores.
11. Pour the cooked candy into a Guttman candy funnel (holds the entire recipe).
- This is the quickest way to get it into molds before it cools and hardens.
12. Spray the funnel with Pam.
13. Pour some candy into molds and the rest onto a marble slab. Roll the marble slab piece into a long cylinder and cut into "pillows" using scissors.
14. Dust finished candies with powdered sugar so they won't melt together.
15. For a nice sucker mold, use a white ring with a slit for the stick (do not spray the stick).
16. Fill the mold, let it cool, and remove easily.
17. Experiment with different flavors; at least 50 oil flavors are available to choose from.
Shared by Dolores McCann, Hamilton OH
